title: Balanced parentheses
description: Check if a string of parentheses is balanced using stack

examples.explanation:
Step 1: String = '(()())', find '()' at positions 2-3 → remove → '(()())' becomes '(())'
Step 2: String = '(())', find '()' at positions 1-2 → remove → '(())' becomes '()'
Step 3: String = '()', find '()' at positions 0-1 → remove → '()' becomes ''
Step 4: String = '' → empty, no '()' found → stop
Result: True (balanced)

Step 1: String = '(()', find '()' at positions 1-2 → remove → '(()' becomes '('
Step 2: String = '(', no '()' found → stop
Result: False (unbalanced, string not empty)

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly remove adjacent matching parentheses pairs from the string until no more can be removed, then check if the string is empty. This is inefficient due to repeated scans and string modifications.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Balanced Parentheses Check via Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution checking if parentheses string is balanced by repeatedly removing adjacent matching pairs
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the balanced parentheses problem using a brute force method that repeatedly scans the string to find and remove adjacent matching parentheses pairs ('()'). The intuition is that a balanced parentheses string can be reduced to an empty string by repeatedly removing these adjacent pairs, similar to how you might simplify nested structures layer by layer. The algorithm works by scanning the string from left to right, looking for any occurrence of '()'. When found, it removes this pair from the string. This process repeats until either the string becomes empty (balanced) or no more '()' pairs can be found but the string isn't empty (unbalanced). The time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case (like '(((...)))'), each removal requires scanning the entire string, and we might need up to n/2 removals. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the string. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as it repeatedly calls the pair removal function until completion. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty strings properly, infinite loops if not checking for pair existence correctly, and inefficient string concatenation. Real code pattern: while True: find '()' in string; if found: remove it; else: break; return len(string) == 0.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String = '(()())', find '()' at positions 2-3 → remove → '(()())' becomes '(())'
Step 2: String = '(())', find '()' at positions 1-2 → remove → '(())' becomes '()'
Step 3: String = '()', find '()' at positions 0-1 → remove → '()' becomes ''
Step 4: String = '' → empty, no '()' found → stop
Result: True (balanced)

Step 1: String = '(()', find '()' at positions 1-2 → remove → '(()' becomes '('
Step 2: String = '(', no '()' found → stop
Result: False (unbalanced, string not empty)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Remove First Adjacent Matching Pair
ladders.desc: Function that scans string to find first '()' pair and returns new string with it removed
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation repeatedly called in the main algorithm. It scans the input string from left to right to find the first occurrence of the substring '()'. When found, it creates and returns a new string with that pair removed. If no '()' is found, it returns the original string unchanged. The intuition is that we're performing one simplification step: removing one layer of matching parentheses from wherever they appear adjacently. This works because in a balanced parentheses sequence, there will always be some adjacent matching pair that can be removed (though not necessarily the first one found). The algorithm: iterate i from 0 to len(s)-2; if s[i] == '(' and s[i+1] == ')', return s[0:i] + s[i+2:]; otherwise return s. Time complexity is O(n) for the scan, and space is O(n) for the new string. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly until no change occurs. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop bounds, not handling empty or single-character strings, and inefficient string building. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)-1): if s[i]=='(' and s[i+1]==')': return s[:i] + s[i+2:]; return s.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String = 'a()b', length=4
Step 2: i=0: 'a' != '(' → continue
Step 3: i=1: s[1]='(', s[2]=')' → match found at positions 1-2
Step 4: Remove positions 1-2: s[0:1]='a' + s[3:]='b' → 'ab'
Result: 'ab'

Step 1: String = '(()', length=3
Step 2: i=0: s[0]='(', s[1]='(' → no match
Step 3: i=1: s[1]='(', s[2]=')' → match found at positions 1-2
Step 4: Remove positions 1-2: s[0:1]='(' + s[3:]='' → '('
Result: '('

ladders.title: Core Logic — Repeated Simplification Until No Change
ladders.desc: Process of repeatedly applying an operation until a fixed point is reached
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves taking an input and repeatedly applying a transformation function until the output stops changing (reaches a fixed point). For balanced parentheses, the transformation is removing adjacent '()' pairs. The intuition is that we keep simplifying the string until it cannot be simplified further, then examine the result. This pattern appears in many algorithms where we iteratively reduce a problem until a base case. The algorithm: current = input; while True: new = transform(current); if new == current: break; current = new; return current. Time complexity depends on the transform function; here it's O(n²) overall. Space is O(n). This connects to L1 by using the pair removal as the transform function. Common mistakes include infinite loops if not comparing properly, not handling the initial state correctly, and inefficient copying. Real code pattern: while True: new_s = remove_pair(s); if new_s == s: break; s = new_s.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with '(()())'
Step 2: Apply transform → '(())' (removed '()' at positions 2-3)
Step 3: Apply transform → '()' (removed '()' at positions 1-2)
Step 4: Apply transform → '' (removed '()' at positions 0-1)
Step 5: Apply transform → '' (no change)
Step 6: Stop, result ''

Step 1: Start with '(()'
Step 2: Apply transform → '(' (removed '()' at positions 1-2)
Step 3: Apply transform → '(' (no change)
Step 4: Stop, result '('

ladders.title: Building Block — String Scanning for Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: Algorithm to find first occurrence of a specific pattern in a string
ladders.explanation: This building block involves scanning through a string to find the first occurrence of a specific substring or pattern. For parentheses balancing, we need to find '()'. The intuition is linear search: we examine each possible starting position in sequence until we find a match or reach the end. This is fundamental to many string processing tasks. The algorithm: for i from 0 to len(s)-len(pattern): check if s[i:i+len(pattern)] equals pattern; if yes, return i; else return -1. For pattern '()' (length 2), we check pairs of adjacent characters. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case, space O(1). This connects to L2 as it's used within the transform function. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds, not handling empty strings, and off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)-1): if s[i]=='(' and s[i+1]==')': return i; return -1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String = 'a()b()c', length=7
Step 2: i=0: s[0]='a', s[1]='(' → no match
Step 3: i=1: s[1]='(', s[2]=')' → match at position 1
Step 4: Return 1

Step 1: String = '(((', length=3
Step 2: i=0: s[0]='(', s[1]='(' → no match
Step 3: i=1: s[1]='(', s[2]='(' → no match
Step 4: No more positions (i max = 1)
Step 5: Return -1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation: Removing a Substring
ladders.desc: Creating a new string with a specified portion removed
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves taking a string and creating a new string that excludes a contiguous segment (substring) at specified positions. For parentheses balancing, we need to remove two characters (a '()' pair). The intuition is string concatenation: we take the part before the removal and the part after, then join them. This is fundamental to immutable string operations in many programming languages. The algorithm: given string s, start index i, and length k to remove, return s[0:i] + s[i+k:]. Time complexity is O(n) as it creates a new string, space O(n). This connects to L3 as it's used after finding a pattern. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling, not understanding string immutability, and inefficient repeated concatenation. Real code pattern: return s[:i] + s[i+2:] for removing 2 chars at i.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String = 'hello world'
Step 2: Remove 5 chars starting at index 6
Step 3: s[0:6] = 'hello '
Step 4: s[6+5:] = s[11:] = 'd'
Step 5: Concatenate: 'hello ' + 'd' = 'hello d'

Step 1: String = 'abc'
Step 2: Remove 1 char at index 1
Step 3: s[0:1] = 'a'
Step 4: s[1+1:] = s[2:] = 'c'
Step 5: Concatenate: 'a' + 'c' = 'ac'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Accessing characters and substrings by position in a string
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding how to work with individual characters and portions of a string using indices (positions). Strings are typically indexed starting from 0, so the first character is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. Slicing allows accessing a substring from a start index (inclusive) to an end index (exclusive). The intuition is that a string is like an array of characters, and we can refer to specific positions to read or extract parts. This is essential for any text processing. Basic operations: s[i] gets character at index i; s[i:j] gets substring from i to j-1; s[i:] gets from i to end; s[:j] gets from start to j-1. Time complexity for indexing is O(1), slicing O(k) where k is slice length. Space for slicing O(k). This connects to L4 as it's used for string removal. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, negative indices confusion, and assuming strings are mutable. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; char = s[1] (gets 'e'); sub = s[1:4] (gets 'ell').

examples.trace:
Step 1: String = 'python'
Step 2: Indices: 0='p', 1='y', 2='t', 3='h', 4='o', 5='n'
Step 3: Slice s[1:4] means from index 1 (inclusive) to 4 (exclusive)
Step 4: Includes indices 1,2,3 → 'y', 't', 'h'
Step 5: Result: 'yth'

Step 1: String = 'abc'
Step 2: Indices: 0='a', 1='b', 2='c'
Step 3: s[0] accesses character at index 0
Step 4: Result: 'a'

approaches.name: Approach 2: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to push opening parentheses and pop when encountering a matching closing parenthesis. If the stack is empty at the end, the string is balanced; otherwise, it's not.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Balanced Parentheses Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to check if a string of parentheses is balanced using a stack data structure.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that for parentheses to be balanced, every opening parenthesis must have a corresponding closing parenthesis in the correct order. A stack's LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) property perfectly matches this requirement because the most recent opening parenthesis must be closed first. We iterate through each character in the string. When we encounter an opening parenthesis ('(', '{', or '['), we push it onto the stack. When we encounter a closing parenthesis (')', '}', or ']'), we check if the stack is empty (which would mean there's no matching opening parenthesis) or if the top of the stack doesn't match the corresponding opening parenthesis. If either condition is true, the string is unbalanced. Otherwise, we pop the matching opening parenthesis from the stack. After processing all characters, if the stack is empty, all opening parentheses have been properly closed, and the string is balanced; otherwise, it's unbalanced. The time complexity is O(n) because we process each character once, and space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when all characters are opening parentheses. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete validation logic that uses the matching check as a subroutine. Common mistakes include not checking for empty stack before popping, forgetting to handle different types of parentheses, and not considering edge cases like empty strings. Real code pattern: def is_balanced(s): stack = []; mapping = {')': '(', '}': '{', ']': '['}; for char in s: if char in mapping.values(): stack.append(char); elif char in mapping: if not stack or stack[-1] != mapping[char]: return False; stack.pop(); return not stack.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize empty stack [].
Step 2: Process '(': push '(' -> stack ['('].
Step 3: Process '{': push '{' -> stack ['(', '{'].
Step 4: Process '[': push '[' -> stack ['(', '{', '['].
Step 5: Process ']': top is '[', matches ']' -> pop '[' -> stack ['(', '{'].
Step 6: Process '}': top is '{', matches '}' -> pop '{' -> stack ['('].
Step 7: Process ')': top is '(', matches ')' -> pop '(' -> stack [].
Step 8: End of string, stack empty -> return True.

Step 1: Initialize empty stack [].
Step 2: Process '(': push '(' -> stack ['('].
Step 3: Process '{': push '{' -> stack ['(', '{'].
Step 4: Process '[': push '[' -> stack ['(', '{', '['].
Step 5: Process ')': top is '[', does not match ')' -> return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Matching Parentheses Check
ladders.desc: Check if a closing parenthesis matches the most recent opening parenthesis from a stack.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in balanced parentheses, each closing parenthesis must correspond to the most recent unmatched opening parenthesis of the same type. This subroutine is the core validation step in the full solution. Given a closing parenthesis character and a stack containing opening parentheses, we first check if the stack is empty—if it is, there's no opening parenthesis to match, so it's invalid. If the stack is not empty, we peek at the top element (the most recent opening parenthesis) and compare it with the expected opening parenthesis for the given closing one. For example, ')' should match '(', '}' should match '{', and ']' should match '['. If they match, we can pop the opening parenthesis; otherwise, it's a mismatch. This operation has O(1) time complexity since it involves constant-time stack operations (peek and comparison), and O(1) space as it uses only the existing stack. It connects to L0 by being the critical check performed for each closing parenthesis in the main loop. Common mistakes include not using a proper mapping dictionary to define matches, incorrectly comparing characters, or forgetting the empty stack check. Real code pattern: def is_matching(closing, stack): mapping = {')': '(', '}': '{', ']': '['}; if not stack: return False; return stack[-1] == mapping[closing].

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check stack not empty -> True.
Step 2: Peek top of stack: '{'.
Step 3: Mapping for '}' is '{'.
Step 4: Compare '{' == '{' -> True.
Step 5: Return True (match found).

Step 1: Check stack not empty -> True.
Step 2: Peek top of stack: '('.
Step 3: Mapping for ']' is '['.
Step 4: Compare '(' == '[' -> False.
Step 5: Return False (mismatch).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Stack-based Parentheses Validation
ladders.desc: Use a stack to track opening parentheses and validate against closing ones.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a stack's LIFO order naturally handles nested structures: the last opening parenthesis must be closed first. This is the central technique of the approach. We process the string character by character. For each character, if it's an opening parenthesis, we push it onto the stack to remember we need to close it later. If it's a closing parenthesis, we check if it matches the top of the stack (using the matching logic from L1). If it matches, we pop the stack, indicating that pair is balanced; if not, the string is unbalanced. After processing all characters, if the stack is empty, all opening parentheses have been matched, and the string is balanced. This logic has O(n) time complexity as we iterate through n characters, each with O(1) stack operations, and O(n) space for the stack in the worst case. It connects to L1 by applying the matching check repeatedly and to L0 by forming the main algorithm loop. Common mistakes include not handling multiple parenthesis types correctly, missing the final stack check, or incorrect iteration order. Real code pattern: def validate_parentheses(s): stack = []; for char in s: if char in '({[': stack.append(char); else: if not stack or not is_matching(char, stack): return False; stack.pop(); return not stack.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack [].
Step 2: Process '(': push '(' -> stack ['('].
Step 3: Process '[': push '[' -> stack ['(', '['].
Step 4: Process ']': top is '[', matches ']' -> pop '[' -> stack ['('].
Step 5: Process ')': top is '(', matches ')' -> pop '(' -> stack [].
Step 6: End, stack empty -> return True.

Step 1: Initialize stack [].
Step 2: Process '(': push '(' -> stack ['('].
Step 3: Process '[': push '[' -> stack ['(', '['].
Step 4: Process ')': top is '[', does not match ')' -> return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Parentheses
ladders.desc: Implement push and pop operations on a stack to manage opening parentheses.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a stack helps track unmatched opening parentheses in order of occurrence. This intermediate concept involves using a list as a stack to store opening parentheses. When we encounter an opening parenthesis ('(', '{', or '['), we push it onto the stack using the append operation, which adds it to the end (simulating the top). When we encounter a closing parenthesis, if it matches the top (as per L2 logic), we pop the stack using pop() to remove the most recent opening parenthesis, indicating it has been closed. The stack thus maintains a history of opening parentheses that are still waiting to be matched. This has O(1) time per operation (push and pop are constant time for lists in Python) and O(n) space in the worst case if all characters are opening parentheses. It connects to L2 by providing the data structure needed for the validation logic. Common mistakes include using incorrect stack methods (e.g., insert instead of append), not handling pop on empty stack, or misunderstanding stack order. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append('(')  # Push; top = stack[-1]  # Peek; stack.pop()  # Pop.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack [].
Step 2: Push '(' -> stack ['('].
Step 3: Push '{' -> stack ['(', '{'].
Step 4: Push '[' -> stack ['(', '{', '['].
Step 5: Pop (remove '[') -> stack ['(', '{'].
Step 6: Pop (remove '{') -> stack ['('].

examples.input: Push '[' then pop from empty stack
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack [].
Step 2: Push '[' -> stack ['['].
Step 3: Pop (remove '[') -> stack [].
Step 4: Attempt pop on empty stack -> error or handle as empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Classification for Parentheses
ladders.desc: Classify a character as opening parenthesis, closing parenthesis, or other.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that we need to distinguish between opening and closing parentheses to apply the correct stack operation. This fundamental operation involves checking each character in the string to determine its type. Opening parentheses are '(', '{', and '['; closing parentheses are ')', '}', and ']'. Any other character (e.g., letters, numbers) is typically ignored or treated as invalid depending on the problem variant. We can use simple conditionals or set membership to classify. For example, if char in '({[', it's an opening parenthesis; if char in ')}]', it's a closing parenthesis. This operation has O(1) time per character and O(1) space, as it uses constant-time checks. It connects to L3 by being the first step in processing each character before deciding to push or pop from the stack. Common mistakes include missing some parenthesis types, incorrect classification logic, or not handling edge cases like empty strings. Real code pattern: def classify_char(char): if char in '({[': return 'opening'; elif char in ')}]': return 'closing'; else: return 'other'.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if '(' is in '({[' -> True.
Step 2: Return 'opening'.

Step 1: Check if '}' is in ')}]' -> True.
Step 2: Return 'closing'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Parentheses Pairs
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that parentheses come in pairs: an opening parenthesis must be followed by a corresponding closing parenthesis of the same type. This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the problem. Parentheses are symbols used in programming and mathematics to group expressions; common types are round parentheses '()', curly braces '{}', and square brackets '[]'. Each type has a specific opening character ('(', '{', '[') and a matching closing character (')', '}', ']'). A pair is balanced if an opening parenthesis is directly or indirectly followed by its matching closing parenthesis, with proper nesting (e.g., '([])' is balanced, but '([)]' is not). This concept has no algorithmic complexity as it's a basic recognition task, but it underpins all higher levels. It connects to L4 by defining what characters to classify. Common mistakes include confusing different parenthesis types or not understanding the pairing requirement. Real code pattern: Pairs are defined as: '(' matches ')', '{' matches '}', '[' matches ']'.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Look at first character '('.
Step 2: It is an opening parenthesis.
Step 3: Look at second character ')'.
Step 4: It is a closing parenthesis that matches '('.
Step 5: They form a valid pair.

Step 1: Look at first character '{'.
Step 2: It is an opening parenthesis.
Step 3: Look at second character '}'.
Step 4: It is a closing parenthesis that matches '{'.
Step 5: They form a valid pair.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Counter O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a counter that increments for opening parentheses and decrements for closing parentheses, ensuring it never goes negative and ends at zero. This works for single-type parentheses but fails for mixed types.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Balanced Parentheses Check with Counter
ladders.desc: Complete solution to check if a string containing only '(' and ')' characters is balanced using a counter approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires determining whether a string of parentheses is balanced, meaning every opening parenthesis '(' has a corresponding closing parenthesis ')' in the correct order. The counter approach provides an elegant O(1) space solution for single-type parentheses. The intuition is simple: we traverse the string while maintaining a balance counter that starts at 0. For each '(' we encounter, we increment the counter (adding to our 'debt' of closing parentheses needed). For each ')' we encounter, we decrement the counter (paying off our debt). The string is balanced if and only if: 1) The counter never goes negative during traversal (ensuring we never close more parentheses than we've opened), and 2) The counter ends at exactly 0 (ensuring all opened parentheses have been properly closed). This approach works because it captures the essential property of balanced parentheses: at any prefix of the string, the number of closing parentheses cannot exceed the number of opening parentheses, and at the end, the counts must be equal. The algorithm: 1) Initialize balance = 0. 2) For each character c in the string: a) If c == '(': balance += 1. b) Else if c == ')': balance -= 1. c) If balance < 0: return False (unbalanced). 3) After loop: return balance == 0. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as we make a single pass. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a single integer variable. This connects to the parent problem of validating nested structures, though it's limited to single-type parentheses. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check for negative balance during traversal (which catches cases like ')(' early), not handling empty strings properly (should return True), and not validating input contains only parentheses characters. The counter approach fails for mixed parentheses types like '[]' or '{}' because it cannot distinguish between different opening/closing pairs.

examples.trace:
Start: balance = 0
Char '(': balance = 1
Char '(': balance = 2
Char ')': balance = 1
Char '(': balance = 2
Char ')': balance = 1
Char ')': balance = 0
End: balance == 0 → True

Start: balance = 0
Char '(': balance = 1
Char ')': balance = 0
Char ')': balance = -1 → Negative detected! Return False immediately
(Note: We don't continue to the last '(' because we return early when balance goes negative)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Balance Counter Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the core traversal logic that updates and validates the balance counter.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the heart of the balanced parentheses solution. The intuition is that we need to process each character sequentially while maintaining a running count that represents our current 'balance' of unmatched opening parentheses. The key insight is that we can validate the string incrementally: as we process each character, we immediately know if the string up to that point is valid. The algorithm: 1) Initialize balance = 0. 2) For each character in the string: a) If character is '(' → increment balance. b) If character is ')' → decrement balance. c) After decrementing, check if balance < 0 → if yes, return False immediately (invalid prefix). 3) After processing all characters, the balance represents the final state. This sub-routine doesn't make the final decision about whether the string is balanced—it just performs the traversal and validation. The time complexity is O(n) for the traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for the balance variable. This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the mechanism to check the two conditions: no negative balance during traversal (checked here) and final balance of 0 (checked in L0 after this sub-routine completes). Common mistakes include: not returning early when balance goes negative (which misses detecting invalid prefixes like ')('), incorrectly handling non-parentheses characters, and off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. The real power of this sub-routine is its early termination capability—we can detect invalidity as soon as it occurs without processing the entire string.

examples.trace:
Start: balance = 0
Char '(': balance = 1
Char '(': balance = 2
Char ')': balance = 1
End: Return balance = 1 (Note: We don't check if balance == 0 here, just return the final balance value)

Start: balance = 0
Char '(': balance = 1
Char ')': balance = 0
Char ')': balance = -1 → Negative detected! Return False immediately
(We terminate early without processing further characters)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Balance Invariant Maintenance
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the balance invariant: counter never negative and ends at zero.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical invariant that makes the counter approach work. The intuition is that a valid parentheses string must satisfy two conditions throughout its entire length: 1) At any point in the string (any prefix), the number of closing parentheses ')' cannot exceed the number of opening parentheses '('. 2) At the end of the string, the total number of opening parentheses must equal the total number of closing parentheses. These two conditions together guarantee that the parentheses are properly nested and balanced. The first condition ensures we never close more parentheses than we've opened (which would create invalid nesting like ')('). The second condition ensures all opened parentheses get closed (avoiding cases like '(()' where some remain open). Mathematically, if we let balance = count('(') - count(')') for any prefix, then condition 1 is balance ≥ 0 for all prefixes, and condition 2 is final balance = 0. The algorithm for checking this invariant: 1) Initialize balance = 0. 2) For each character: update balance accordingly. 3) Track two boolean flags: never_negative (stays True if balance never goes below 0) and final_zero (True if balance ends at 0). 4) The string is valid if both flags are True. Time complexity is O(n), space is O(1). This core logic connects to L1 (the traversal) by providing the specific conditions to check during and after traversal. Common mistakes include: checking only the final balance without verifying the never-negative condition (would incorrectly accept ')('), or checking only the never-negative condition without verifying final zero (would incorrectly accept '(()'). Understanding this two-part invariant is crucial—it's why the counter approach works and what distinguishes it from simply counting total parentheses.

examples.trace:
Start: balance = 0, never_negative = True
Char '(': balance = 1 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
Char ')': balance = 0 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
Char '(': balance = 1 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
Char ')': balance = 0 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
End: final_zero = (balance == 0) = True
Result: Both conditions satisfied

Start: balance = 0, never_negative = True
Char '(': balance = 1 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
Char '(': balance = 2 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
Char ')': balance = 1 (≥0, never_negative stays True)
End: final_zero = (balance == 0) = False
Result: Fails final_zero condition

ladders.title: Building Block — Character Classification and Counter Update
ladders.desc: Process individual parentheses characters and update a counter based on character type.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operation of classifying a character as either an opening or closing parenthesis and updating a counter accordingly. The intuition is simple: we need to distinguish between two types of characters and perform opposite operations on our balance counter. For the balanced parentheses problem with only '(' and ')', the classification is straightforward: if the character is '(', it's an opening parenthesis and should increase our balance (increment counter). If the character is ')', it's a closing parenthesis and should decrease our balance (decrement counter). The algorithm: 1) Receive a character as input. 2) Check if character equals '(' → if yes, return +1 (increment signal). 3) Check if character equals ')' → if yes, return -1 (decrement signal). 4) (Optional) Handle invalid characters by returning an error or 0. The time complexity is O(1) per character. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 (balance invariant) by providing the atomic operations that build up the balance value. Each character processing contributes either +1 or -1 to the running balance. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases like empty strings or strings with non-parentheses characters, confusing which character causes increment vs decrement, and not considering case sensitivity (though parentheses are typically case-sensitive in these problems). This building block is deceptively simple but crucial—it's where the actual 'work' of the algorithm happens for each character. In the full algorithm, we'll call this classification for each character in the string and accumulate the results.

examples.trace: Character is '(' → Classification: opening parenthesis → Action: increment balance → Return value: +1
Character is ')' → Classification: closing parenthesis → Action: decrement balance → Return value: -1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Increment/Decrement
ladders.desc: Perform basic arithmetic operations on an integer counter variable.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses the most fundamental operation needed for the counter approach: modifying an integer variable through increment and decrement operations. The intuition is that we need a way to keep track of a changing numerical value as we process the string. The balance counter is simply an integer that starts at 0. When we encounter an opening parenthesis '(', we need to increase the counter by 1 (increment). When we encounter a closing parenthesis ')', we need to decrease the counter by 1 (decrement). These are basic arithmetic operations: increment is typically implemented as counter = counter + 1 or counter += 1, while decrement is counter = counter - 1 or counter -= 1. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an integer variable (let's call it 'balance') to 0. 2) To increment: balance = balance + 1. 3) To decrement: balance = balance - 1. 4) To check if negative: balance < 0. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the integer variable. This connects to L3 (character classification) because once we classify a character, we apply the corresponding arithmetic operation to our counter. Common mistakes include: using the wrong operator (e.g., incrementing when should decrement), forgetting to initialize the counter to 0, and integer overflow (not a concern for reasonable string lengths). This basic operation is so simple that it's easy to overlook, but it's the mechanical heart of the algorithm—without reliable increment/decrement operations, the entire approach falls apart. In programming languages, these operations are typically atomic and highly optimized.

examples.trace:
Initial balance: 2
Apply decrement operation: balance = balance - 1
Calculate: 2 - 1 = 1
New balance: 1

Initial balance: 0
Apply increment operation: balance = balance + 1
Calculate: 0 + 1 = 1
New balance: 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Traversal and Character Access
ladders.desc: Iterate through a string and access individual characters sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental prerequisite concept: how to traverse a string character by character. The intuition is that to process a string of parentheses, we need to examine each character in order from left to right. A string is essentially a sequence of characters, and we need a way to access each one sequentially. The most basic algorithm is: 1) Start at the beginning of the string (position 0). 2) Access the character at the current position. 3) Process that character (in our case, eventually we'll classify it as opening or closing). 4) Move to the next position. 5) Repeat until we reach the end of the string. In pseudocode, this is typically done with a loop: for i in range(len(string)): char = string[i] or more simply: for char in string: process(char). Time complexity for traversing a string of length n is O(n) since we visit each character once. Space complexity is O(1) for the traversal itself (not counting any processing storage). This connects to L4 (integer operations) because once we can access each character, we'll use that character to decide whether to increment or decrement our counter. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (accessing string[len(string)] which is out of bounds), confusing string indices (usually 0-based), and not handling empty strings (which should result in zero iterations). This foundation is essential because without the ability to traverse a string, we cannot implement any algorithm that processes input data sequentially. Even the simplest string processing task requires this basic skill. For complete beginners, understanding that strings are indexable sequences and that loops allow systematic access to each element is a critical first step.

examples.trace:
String length: 3
Position 0: character = 'a'
Position 1: character = 'b'
Position 2: character = 'c'
End of string reached

String length: 0
Empty string → no positions to access
Immediately finished
